title: Needle-felted Christmas ornament
subtitle: A hanging ball approximately 5 cm across, wrapped in merino roving and decorated with needle-felted colour bands and dots
excerpt: Wrap a polystyrene ball core in merino roving, needle-felt the surface until firm, then add colour bands and dot decorations with fine needles. The finished ornament is approximately 5 cm in diameter and hangs from a loop of twisted wool. A compact project that uses very small quantities of roving.
sourceNotes: Observation-led; foam-core needle-felted ornaments are a standard introductory needle-felting project used in craft teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres aligned in a continuous rope or strip, ready for spinning or felting. In needle felting, thin wisps are pulled from roving and pressed onto a surface using a barbed needle, which tangles the fibres together without heat or water.

A dry felting method in which barbed needles are stabbed repeatedly into loose wool fibres, tangling them together mechanically without water or heat. The barbs on the needle catch the fibres and push them deeper into the surface with each stroke, compressing the wool into a dense, stable fabric or three-dimensional form.

content.text: to wrap a polystyrene ball in merino

and then add surface decoration. Polystyrene provides a stable core that keeps the ball perfectly round and means you do not have to build a three-dimensional form from scratch. The decorative colour bands and dots are felted directly into the surface with a fine needle once the base coat is firm. The finished ball is approximately 5 cm in diameter; scale up by starting with a larger polystyrene ball and proportionally more roving.
Felting needles are extremely sharp and break easily. Stab straight in and out; never lever sideways. Keep fingers clear of the needle path.

attrs.name: Merino roving, base colour (such as cream or red), 15 g
Merino roving, 2 contrast colours for decoration, 3 g each
Felting foam mat or block, at least 10 x 10 cm
Palm fitter (to hold and rotate the ball safely while felting)
Length of twisted wool or ribbon, 20 cm, for the hanging loop

content.text: Pull a thin strip of base-colour roving approximately 2 cm wide and long enough to wrap around the ball once with a little overlap. Wrap the strip around the ball at the equator, overlapping the ends. Begin needling through the wrap and into the polystyrene with the 38-gauge needle: straight in and straight out, about 5 mm deep. Work in a small area for 20 to 30 stabs until the wool starts to compress, then move to the adjacent area. The polystyrene accepts the needle easily and holds the fibres in place as you work. Rotate the ball continuously as you wrap and needle so that the first layer covers the ball evenly. Add further strips over the poles, needling each strip down before adding the next. When the entire ball surface is covered in a thin but even layer, begin building up a second coat in the same way.
Step 2: Build up the base coat

Continue adding thin strips of base-colour roving and needling each one down until the ball has a dense, even surface with no polystyrene visible beneath the wool. The surface should feel firm when pressed with a fingertip, with no springiness from the polystyrene underneath. Three to four full coverage passes is typical. Any thin or patchy areas will be visible in the finished decoration; patch them now by laying a small wisp over the area and needling it in. Keep rotating the ball throughout to maintain even density.

Pull a thin wisp of the first contrast-colour roving and lay it around the ball's equator as a narrow band, roughly 5 mm wide. Needle it in with the fine 38-gauge needle using precise vertical stabs along the length of the band. The 38-gauge needle gives more control than a coarser needle at this scale; the edges of the colour band will be cleanest if you needle along the inner and outer boundaries separately rather than working across the whole band at once. Add a second band at each pole: lay the wisp as a circle covering the top 1.5 cm of the ball and needle it down to form a circular cap at each end.

Pull tiny pinches of the second contrast-colour roving and roll each one between finger and thumb into a small ball. Place each ball on the surface of the ornament and needle it down with 10 to 15 stabs from the 38-gauge needle, keeping the needle perpendicular to the surface at all times. Space dots evenly between the colour bands. Six to eight dots evenly placed around the middle section of the ball looks balanced. Each dot should be roughly 8 mm in diameter when felted down flat; start larger and needle to the desired size.

Push the felting needle through the ball at the top pole to create a channel. Thread the hanging cord through the channel so that both ends extend from the same entry point, then tie the ends in a knot to form a loop. Pull the knot tight against the surface of the ball. If the knot slips into the polystyrene, place a small flat bead or button on the cord below the knot to act as a stop. Alternatively, fold the cord in half, push the looped end through the ball with a blunt darning needle, and thread the two free ends through the loop before pulling tight: this produces a lark's-head loop with no knot visible at the top.

attrs.cause: The needle was levered sideways rather than pulled straight out, or it struck the dense core of the polystyrene at an angle
attrs.fix: Always enter and exit in the same line. If resistance is felt, do not push harder; withdraw the needle and reposition slightly. Keep spare needles — they do break and it is not a mistake, just a material reality.

attrs.problem: The colour band has ragged or fuzzy edges
attrs.cause: The roving wisp was too thick, or the needle was working across the boundary rather than along it
attrs.fix: Use the finest possible wisp for decoration. Needle along each edge of the band separately to define a clear boundary, working from the centre of the band outward to the edge rather than across.

attrs.problem: The polystyrene core is visible through the base coat
attrs.cause: The roving was applied in thick clumps that felted unevenly, leaving thin gaps between them
attrs.fix: Use very thin wisps for each pass and build up many thin layers rather than fewer thick ones. Patch visible polystyrene by laying a tiny wisp of roving directly over the area and needling it down with careful vertical stabs.
